title: Monopoly 10x Thinking

content.statement: Aim for a 10x improvement that creates a monopoly position.
content.question: On what one dimension can I credibly become 10x better than alternatives?
content.equation: Monopoly Position = (Performance Multiple + Switching Cost + Defensibility) ÷ 3
content.mental_model: Canyon, no one can casually cross it.
content.business_lens: On the dimension customers care about most, aim for 10x not 10%; protect with structural moats.
content.life_lens: Pursue work where you can be order-of-magnitude better, not marginally better.
sop: Identify the dimension customers care about most
Set a 10x target on that dimension
Build durable advantages (network effects, scale, IP, brand)
Protect the moat as you grow
Reinvest profits into widening the gap
content.explainer: PayPal’s online payment ease and security was so much better than checks for eBay sellers that it locked in a dominant position. Thiel argues most successful companies start by being best at something narrow, not slightly better at everything.
questions: On what one dimension can I credibly become 10x better than alternatives?
If a customer had to explain in one sentence why switching to a competitor isn't worth it, what would they say, and is that sentence true today?
Which of your current advantages could a well-funded competitor copy within six months, and which one genuinely couldn't?

definitions.def: A durable competitive advantage that protects your business from competition over time. Like a castle moat, keeps rivals out.
The friction, cost, or effort a customer would have to absorb to leave you for a competitor.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Performance Multiple 8 (how much better on the dimension customers care most about), Switching Cost 5, Defensibility 6. Score = (8 + 5 + 6) ÷ 3 = 6.3. Higher means competitors structurally can't catch up soon; lower means your edge is marginal and easily matched.
content.closing_statement: Ten percent better gets copied; ten times doesn't.

caption:
Principle Nº 294: Monopoly 10x Thinking

Aim for a 10x improvement that creates a monopoly position. PayPal’s online payment ease and security was so much better than checks for eBay sellers that it locked in a dominant position.

Ask yourself: On what one dimension can I credibly become 10x better than alternatives?
